Tip - Something I did when built mine (similar to yours, not 100%) - Most people wont start with a blank folder, you will have a history of old project folders, full of XLs, PDFs, DOCs, JPGs, etc that you will want to keep as part of this OS as it may provide context to a particular task. Pre-organize and clean those folders manually a bit first, but then run skill creating what you call here the memory.md but asking to form a cache of what is in those old files. You will save a lot of tokens (and time) later just finding what you need in that MD file then having cowork running a bunch of scripts to find the right PDF where that info you are looking for resides.

transcript: Hey co-work, I am hung over. What are we supposed to be doing today? Finalize outline for workshop, dinner, newsletter, right? Um, review expenses. Um, how much did I spend last month? Process last month's credit card statement. My spending is about a,000% mainly from Bumble Premium. Good job throwing money away while not landing a single. That's unnecessarily harsh. Did I Did I really spend that much? Oh, all right. Moving on. Um, finalize my next newsletter using my tone of voice and just link me to the notion page. I'm I'm too hung over to do anything today. Okay, final copy under final copy section. All right, let's open the link. Okay, this is my rough draft up here. And final copy there. cuz you probably missed the tip one. All right. Not bad. Not bad. Okay. Uh, not bad. That was easy. Um, I'm going back to bed. Thanks. Oh, no. Going back to bed. What the All right. I'm not actually hung over, but what Co-work did is real, and it's insane what it can do with the right setup. And in this video, I'll walk you through how to build that setup without burning through your rate limits. Let's get started. So, how did Coowork actually do all of that? Let me show you what happened behind the scenes. Here you can see I named my parent folder iCloud OS because get it, it's supposed to be iCloud, but we're using Claude, so it's iCloud. I'm so funny. Anyways, um whenever I start a new session, Co-work will always first read this claw.md file, which is essentially a set of master rules for co-work. Then co-work will read the memory.mmd file to remember what we did last time. So these two files together, cloudmd and memory.mmd run my entire cowwork workspace. And here's the single most important takeaway from this video. These are just simple text files. So don't be intimidated. If I click this open, you're like, "Oh my god, this looks like code." It's not. I can select all this text, copy, open up any notes app, paste. And I'm just going to scroll all the way back up. And all of a sudden, you realize it's not that scary. Header one, Jeff's co-work. Uh, this is the central hub for all projects, planning, and work sessions. And underneath, it's simple text. The crazy thing is you don't even need to write any of this text yourself because Co-Work writes all of this for you. And by the end of this video, you'll see just how simple this system is. For example, within my rootclaw.mmd file, there is an instruction here that says at the start of every session, readmemory.mmd before responding. So, let's open up the memory.md file. And what do you know? There is a section right at the top here called active projects and work followed by a list of all the active projects I'm working on. That's how coor knows what I'm working on right now. It's the same idea with my tone of voice. Within the claw.md file, there's a section called content and production where the rule is saying, "Hey, read this Jeff's voice principles markdown file before outputting anything to Jeff." Right? And that's how my newsletter goes from rough talking points to a final draft sounding like me. In plain English, claw.md is the instruction manual that tells co-work how to behave and it points to other files whenever it needs more detail. That's it. That's the entire system. To really help you understand, here's a simple analogy. The United States is governed by a set of countrywide laws called the US Constitution that apply to every person in every state. Theoretically, this is level zero, the root level, countrywide laws. Moving one level down, we have state laws. For example, California has its own laws that help tech billionaires get even richer. And New York, not to be outdone, has New York state laws helping the finance billionaires get even richer. I'm just kidding with the laws, obviously. Not really. But the point is, every state has state level laws that stack on top of the US Constitution. So, a business in California is bound by the countrywide constitution plus

hook_text_english: 3 (more) common AI terms most people don't know
full_text_english:
3 (more) common #AI terms most people don’t know:

Term 1: Grounding.
Without it, AI answers from memory, which may or may not be correct. Hand it the actual document and say "answer from this," and now the response is grounded in something real. Whenever accuracy matters, give the AI something to reference.

Term 2: RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation).
Without RAG, the AI writes from memory. With RAG, it goes to the library first, pulls the relevant sources, then generates its answer. The rule of thumb: if an AI tool cites its sources, it most likely uses #RAG. If it doesn't, it's guessing.

Term 3: Context engineering.
Prompt engineering focuses on writing a good prompt (task, context, tone). Context engineering focuses on assembling everything the AI needs: background, examples, constraints. Spend your time on the context, not the wording.
body_summary_english: The video explains Grounding (referencing actual documents for accuracy), RAG (retrieving sources before generating answers), and how to identify AI tools using these techniques.
comments: TᕼᗩᑎK YOᑌ 🙏for all of your assistance. You did a great job 👍
When to use RAG vs MCP? MCP is just a connector to data, RAG is still needed to pull the content?
mantap!
title: Stop perfecting your prompt. Do this instead
caption:
3 (more) common #AI terms most people don’t know:

Term 1: Grounding.
Without it, AI answers from memory, which may or may not be correct. Hand it the actual document and say "answer from this," and now the response is grounded in something real. Whenever accuracy matters, give the AI something to reference.

Term 2: RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation).
Without RAG, the AI writes from memory. With RAG, it goes to the library first, pulls the relevant sources, then generates its answer. The rule of thumb: if an AI tool cites its sources, it most likely uses #RAG. If it doesn't, it's guessing.

Term 3: Context engineering.
Prompt engineering focuses on writing a good prompt (task, context, tone). Context engineering focuses on assembling everything the AI needs: background, examples, constraints. Spend your time on the context, not the wording.
transcript: Common AI terms simply explain part two. First, grounding. Think of AI without grounding like asking a friend to answer from memory. They might be right, they might be not, but hand them the actual document and say, "Answer from this." And now their answer is grounded in something real. The implication here is whenever accuracy matters, make sure the AI has something to reference. Second, RAG, which stands for retrieval augmented generation. Imagine a student writing an essay. Without RAG, the student writes everything from memory. With RAG, the student goes to the library first, pulls the relevant books, then writes the essay based on what they found. The rule of thumb is if an AI tool cites its sources, it most likely uses RAG. If it doesn't, it's writing from memory. Third, context engineering. Here's a simple sidebyside. Prompt engineering is putting the right components together, task, context, tone to write a good prompt. Context engineering is more about giving the AI everything it needs to work with, background, examples, constraints, and less about the wording of the prompt. So instead of spending time perfecting your question, spend it assembling the right context.

Term 2: RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation).
Without RAG, the AI writes from memory. With RAG, it goes to the library first, pulls the relevant sources, then generates its answer. The rule of thumb: if an AI tool cites its sources, it most likely uses #RAG. If it doesn't, it's guessing.

Term 3: Context engineering.
Prompt engineering focuses on writing a good prompt (task, context, tone). Context engineering focuses on assembling everything the AI needs: background, examples, constraints. Spend your time on the context, not the wording.

hook_text_english: AI hallucination is the flip side of creativity
full_text_english:
#AI hallucination is the flip side of creativity. That's the first of three terms most people misunderstand.

Term 1: Hallucination. The same mechanism that produces wrong answers is what generates creative writing, ideas, and novel connections. The real question is whether your task needs creativity or accuracy. Turning off web search in #ChatGPT or Claude gives you more creativity. Turning it on gives you more accuracy.

Term 2: Context window. Think of it as a fixed-size whiteboard. Once it's full, the AI drops older information to fit new input. If your AI starts giving weird answers mid-conversation, just start a new chat.

Term 3: Tokens. This is why free plans have usage limits. AI breaks your words into chunks called tokens, and every chunk costs usage. Better prompts mean fewer tokens, so you get more out of your plan before hitting the cap.
body_summary_english: Educational post clarifying three misunderstood AI terms: hallucination, context window, and another concept. Explains the tradeoffs between creativity and accuracy in different AI tools.
comments:
1) Hallucination is good for imaginary stuff like story of something new in any form. Images of something new. Everywhere else is just creating inexistent stuff that you have to fix.
2) Context window is tied to tokens and limited by first layer of AI model - how many numbers it can consume.
3) AI is math, and math works with numbers, so everything is converted into numbers, where usual words and phrases has lower numbers (usually). Those numbers are called tokens.,Is there a recommended way(s) of framing context from one chat to another. ie if you've exceeded thr context window from one chat and you need to start a new one, what are some effective/efficient ways of reusing context from the prior chat?,You must be lucky to have google subed to you,What are better ways to structure prompts? For example, if Claude looks at your input and, instead of focusing on one objective, you give it three or four tasks in a single prompt, does that reduce usage credits?

Is the usage based more on the number of requests sent to Claude rather than the total amount of text included in the prompt?,His

hook_text_english: Here are the 3 biggest shifts in how you work
full_text_english:
#ClaudeCowork is the next level up from #Claude Chat. Here are the 3 biggest shifts in how you work.

Shift 1: Claude Chat limits you to 20 files per conversation and 30MB per file, since everything uploads to the cloud. Claude Cowork reads directly from your computer, so there's no file limit and no size cap.

Shift 2: Claude Chat has a much smaller context window, meaning it starts compacting your conversation and losing important details way sooner than Claude Cowork.

Shift 3: Claude Chat gi
body_summary_english: Compares Claude Cowork vs Claude Chat across 3 dimensions: file handling limits, context window size, and additional capabilities. Positions Cowork as the superior tool for power users.

hook_text_english: If you're prompting ClaudeCowork the same way, you're doing it wrong
full_text_english:
If you're prompting #ClaudeCowork the same way you prompt #Claude Chat, you're doing it wrong.

Claude Chat uses task-first language. You guide every step: "Do this first. Now format it like that."

Cowork uses outcome-first language. You describe the end result and let it figure out the approach.

For example, with Claude Chat: "I have 15 raw thumbnail images. What naming convention would you recommend?"

You get a recommendation, then do the work yourself.

With Claude Cowork on the other hand
body_summary_english: Explains the fundamental difference in prompting strategy between Claude Chat (task-first) and Claude Cowork (outcome-first). Shows practical example of how the approach differs.

hook_text_english: The trick is figuring out what matters without reading everything
full_text_english:
Most of what you upload to #NotebookLM won't make it into your final output. The trick is figuring out what matters without reading everything.

That's where Mind Maps come in.

Instead of scanning through every source, you generate one map and instantly see every topic and subtopic laid out visually, allowing you to cherry-pick what's relevant and skip the rest.

Since my audience cares a LOT more about practical tips as opposed to technical stuff, I clicked "11 Practical Techniques" on the min
body_summary_english: Tips for using NotebookLM's Mind Maps feature to filter source materials by relevance without manually reviewing all content. Practical efficiency hack for report generation.

full_text_english:
✅ Grab HubSpot’s NotebookLM guide: https://clickhubspot.com/414d85

#NotebookLM just got a massive wave of updates and is now more popular than #Gemini itself. In this full walkthrough, I break down the three-column layout, rank every Studio panel tool into Tier 1 (must-use) and Tier 2 (situational), and share the exact prompt templates and workflows I use to turn raw sources into finished reports, presentations, and social-ready visuals.

*TIMESTAMPS*
00:00 NotebookLM Full Tutorial
00:21 Notebo
body_summary_english: Full walkthrough of NotebookLM updates including three-column layout, tool rankings, and prompt templates for converting sources into reports and presentations. Positions tool against Gemini.

hook_text_english: NotebookLM now has more search interest than Google Gemini
full_text_english:
#NotebookLM now has more search interest than even Google Gemini.

Its core advantage is the same as before though: you throw in documents across different formats and get a grounded answer instead of hallucinations.

Use it when you know which sources have the answers, those sources are in different formats, and accuracy actually matters.
body_summary_english: Announces NotebookLM's rising popularity and explains its core strength: grounded answers from diverse document formats when accuracy matters. Positions it for source-heavy workflows.

hook_text_english: The draft is maybe 20% of the work
full_text_english:
️Try Gamma now ➡️ https://gam.link/jeffsu

Most #AI presentation tutorials stop at the first draft. But if you've worked in any corporate environment, you know the draft is maybe 20% of the work. The other 80% is rounds of edits from stakeholders who all want different things.

This video walks through a real workflow using #Gamma, covering seven realistic editing scenarios based on actual stakeholder roles: reordering narrative structure, adding slides with live data, verifying stats, transform
body_summary_english: Walkthrough of realistic Gamma editing workflows for 7 stakeholder scenarios beyond first draft creation. Addresses the 80% of presentation work that involves iteration and revisions.

hook_text_english: I use all three AI tools daily. Here's why.
full_text_english:
I use all three AI tools daily. Here's why.

ChatGPT follows complex instructions better than anything else. Gemini processes video, audio, and images natively. Claude writes first drafts that barely need editing. They're not interchangeable.

My actual workflow: ChatGPT and Gemini handle ideation and research. Claude handles the final polish.
body_summary_english: Authority-based comparison of ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude with distinct use cases: instruction-following, multimodal processing, and draft quality respectively. Explains integrated daily workflow.

hook_text_english: Google's AI Course on Coursera: 4 research-backed skills to get ahead
full_text_english:
✅ Google’s #AI Course on Coursera: https://imp.i384100.net/YVD5vq

Knowing how to "use AI" is now table stakes (unfortunately) 😅. This video covers 4 skills that will help you get ahead, and each skill is backed by research from Wharton, Harvard, BCG, and MIT.

You'll learn a mental model for deciding when to delegate, collaborate, or go manual with AI; how to design workflows that compound as AI improves; why storytelling remains your strongest moat; and how to keep your critical thinking shar
body_summary_english: Learn AI delegation, workflow design, storytelling, and critical thinking through research from top institutions. Skills are backed by Wharton, Harvard, BCG, and MIT.

hook_text_english: After 3 years testing AI tools daily: the one thing each does better
full_text_english:
AI Systems Course: https://systemsacademy.ai/?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=video&utm_campaign=Q1_CNT_YOUTUBE_VIDEO_198_AI

After three years of testing AI tools daily, I've narrowed down the one thing each tool does better than every alternative.

This is Part 2 of the AI Superpower Series, covering Productivity AI and Creative AI. You'll see exactly when to use Google Workspace's #Gemini over standalone chatbots, what Notion AI can actually do inside your workspace, and how #Midjourney, Nano B
body_summary_english: Part 2 of AI Superpower Series covers Productivity and Creative AI. Breaks down when to use Gemini, Notion AI, Midjourney, and other tools based on daily testing.

hook_text_english: Every AI tool excels at one thing: ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Perplexity compared
full_text_english:
⚡️ HubSpot’s Free Guide: https://clickhubspot.com/5c4cca

Every AI tool excels at one thing. #ChatGPT follows complex instructions without dropping steps. #Gemini handles video, audio, and massive files other models can't process. #Claude produces working code and polished copy on the first try. Perplexity fetches accurate information in seconds. NotebookLM answers only from your sources, so it can't hallucinate.

This video breaks down when to use each tool based on three years of daily use, fi
body_summary_english: Compares AI tools by their strengths: ChatGPT for complex instructions, Gemini for multimodal files, Claude for code/copy, Perplexity for research, NotebookLM for source accuracy.

hook_text_english: Six AI trends backed by data from McKinsey, Stanford, OpenAI, Epoch AI
full_text_english:
Most #AI predictions are speculation. This video covers six trends backed by data from McKinsey, Stanford, #OpenAI, and Epoch AI, with specific steps you can take for each one.

You'll learn why the "best model" debate no longer matters, how AI workflows are outperforming autonomous agents in real enterprise settings, and why your file management system now directly impacts how useful AI can be for your work. Each trend includes concrete examples and a clear action item.

*TIMESTAMPS*
00:00 Top
body_summary_english: Covers data-backed AI trends including why model debates no longer matter, AI workflows outperforming autonomous agents, and file management's impact on AI utility. Includes concrete examples and action items.
